skill.name: adr-log
skill.description: Document architecture decisions with ADR (Architecture Decision Records). Use when making significant technical decisions, choosing between alternatives, or when onboarding needs context on past decisions.
skill.summary: Create Architecture Decision Records that capture context, alternatives, decisions, and trade-offs.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed in context and are not confirmed malicious. The high weak-crypto and entropy alerts are false positives from prose, Korean text, and markdown content. The only confirmed risks are low: Bash is allowed and the skill includes optional CLI examples plus external reference links.

low_findings.title: Optional Shell Commands in Documentation
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE as low risk. The skill declares Bash as an allowed tool and includes adr-tools installation and usage commands. The commands are documentation examples for a legitimate ADR workflow, but users should review them before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash permission and command examples are directly present. They are limited to installing and using adr-tools, with no evidence of hidden command construction.

low_findings.title: External Reference Links
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE as low risk. The skill links to official or relevant documentation pages. These URLs are references, not automatic network calls or exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plainly visible markdown links. No code sends data to those links or fetches them automatically.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and Entropy Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts match ordinary prose or generated report text, not cryptographic API usage. The entropy alert is consistent with multilingual markdown content, not binary or encrypted payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptions, marketplace text, and FAQ content. I found no calls to MD5, SHA1, DES, or any cryptographic library.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified as Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Most reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are markdown code fences, inline file names, and sample ADR text. They do not execute commands inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context shows fenced markdown blocks and inline markdown. There is no Ruby source file and no backtick command substitution logic.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose the reasons behind major technical decisions. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create clear ADRs with context, options, and trade-offs.
actual_capabilities: Creates ADR templates with status, context, decision, alternatives, and consequences.
Writes sample ADRs for technology choices such as frameworks or databases.
Suggests ADR numbering ranges for frontend, backend, infrastructure, and security decisions.
Builds README-style ADR indexes with title, status, and date fields.
Explains when an ADR is useful during reviews, onboarding, and planning.
limitations: Does not verify that a technical decision is correct for your system.
Does not automatically inspect repository architecture unless you provide context.
Includes optional CLI examples, but does not require external tools.
Provides documentation guidance, not legal or compliance approval.

use_cases.title: Record platform decisions
use_cases.description: Capture why a framework, database, or architecture pattern was selected over alternatives.

use_cases.title: Improve team memory
use_cases.description: Give new team members concise context for decisions made before they joined.

use_cases.title: Track infrastructure choices
use_cases.description: Document deployment, CI/CD, observability, and infrastructure trade-offs in a consistent format.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic ADR
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an ADR for choosing [decision] in [project]. Include context, decision, alternatives, consequences, and status.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Alternatives
prompt_templates.prompt: Write an ADR comparing [option A], [option B], and [option C]. Explain the chosen option and the main trade-offs.

prompt_templates.title: Update Existing ADR
prompt_templates.prompt: Revise this ADR because [new information]. Mark the old decision as superseded and describe the replacement decision.

prompt_templates.title: Build ADR System
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an ADR workflow for this repository. Include folder structure, numbering rules, review steps, and README index format.

output_examples.input: Create an ADR for choosing PostgreSQL instead of MongoDB for billing data.
output: Decision: Use PostgreSQL for billing and relational user data.
Context: Billing requires transactions, referential integrity, and complex reporting.
Alternatives: MongoDB was considered for flexible documents but rejected for weaker relational fit.
Consequences: Strong consistency improves reliability, while schema migrations need planning.

output_examples.input: Add an ADR index entry for choosing Next.js App Router.
output: Summary: Selected App Router for server components, nested layouts, and improved data loading.

output_examples.input: Create a superseding ADR for replacing JWT sessions with server sessions.
output: Reason: Server sessions reduce token exposure and simplify revocation.
Migration impact: Clients need session cookie handling and revised logout behavior.

best_practices: Write ADRs while the decision context is still fresh.
Record serious alternatives, including options the team rejected.
Document risks and follow-up work as clearly as benefits.
anti_patterns: Writing ADRs only after implementation to justify a finished choice.
Leaving every ADR in Proposed status after decisions are made.
Using ADRs for small code changes that do not affect architecture.

faq.question: What is an ADR?
faq.answer: An ADR is an Architecture Decision Record. It documents a significant technical decision and the reason behind it.

faq.question: When should I create one?
faq.answer: Create one for decisions that are costly to reverse, affect multiple teams, or need future explanation.

faq.question: Can this skill use Claude Code or Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require adr-tools?
faq.answer: No. The skill includes adr-tools examples, but plain markdown files are enough.

faq.question: Can it update an existing ADR?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft superseding or deprecated decision records when you provide the existing context.

faq.question: Does it make architecture decisions for me?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure the decision record. Humans still need to evaluate project constraints.
